title: MemPrune: Privacy-Enhancing Neural Network Pruning via Memorization-Aware Gradient Dispersion Criterion
description: We propose MemPrune, a pruning method that uses per-weight Gradient Dispersion Score (GDS)---the variance of per-sample gradients---to identify and remove weights that contribute to memorization rather than generalization. Grounded in the Coherent Gradients Hypothesis (high per-weight gradient variance indicates incoherent, sample-specific updates characteristic of memorization), MemPrune creates a pruning criterion explicitly optimized for privacy enhancement. Unlike prior pruning-privacy work that analyzes standard pruning through a DP lens (Huang et al., 2020), modifies retraining (Shang et al., 2025), or uses standard criteria (Wang et al., 2021), MemPrune targets the pruning criterion itself, simultaneously achieving model compression and privacy protection.
motivation: Standard neural network pruning techniques (magnitude-based, gradient sensitivity) increase vulnerability to membership inference attacks because they force remaining weights to memorize training data more tightly (Yuan et al., USENIX 2022). While Huang et al. (2020) proved pruning is theoretically equivalent to adding DP noise, and Adamczewski & Park (2023) used pruning to improve DP-SGD scalability, no existing work designs a pruning criterion specifically to remove memorization-contributing weights. Current defenses modify the retraining process post-pruning (Shang et al., NDSS 2025) but do not address the root cause: the pruning criterion itself is agnostic to whether a weight encodes generalizable knowledge or memorized information.
proposed_approach: We introduce the Gradient Dispersion Score (GDS), computed as the variance of per-sample gradients for each weight at a single checkpoint. This adapts the gradient-variance-as-memorization-signal from Agarwal et al. (2022) from per-sample difficulty estimation to per-weight pruning: weights with high GDS respond very differently to individual training samples, indicating they encode sample-specific (memorized) patterns. The theoretical grounding comes from the Coherent Gradients Hypothesis (Chatterjee, 2020): SGD gradients are strongest in coherent directions that reduce loss on many examples, so weights driven by incoherent (high-variance) gradients serve memorization. MemPrune removes high-GDS weights first, directly excising memorization from the network. The method has three phases: (1) compute per-weight GDS using a subset of training data with chunked vmap computation for memory efficiency, (2) prune weights with highest layer-normalized GDS, (3) fine-tune with optional DP-SGD for formal guarantees. We validate GDS as a memorization signal via label-noise canary injection with ground-truth memorized samples.

related_work.title: Membership Inference Attacks and Defenses in Neural Network Pruning
related_work.relevance: First systematic study showing pruning increases MIA vulnerability. Uses magnitude pruning and proposes regularization-based defenses. We propose a fundamentally different approach: changing the pruning criterion itself.

related_work.title: Defending Against Membership Inference Attacks on Iteratively Pruned Deep Neural Networks
related_work.authors: Jing Shang, Jian Wang, Kailun Wang, Jiqiang Liu, Nan Jiang, Md Armanuzzaman, Ziming Zhao
related_work.relevance: Proposes WeMem framework to defend during iterative pruning by reducing data reuse. Modifies retraining process, not the pruning criterion. Complementary to our approach.

related_work.title: Privacy-preserving Learning via Deep Net Pruning
related_work.authors: Yangsibo Huang, Yushan Su, Sachin Ravi, Zhao Song, Sanjeev Arora, Kai Li
related_work.relevance: Proves pruning is theoretically equivalent to adding DP noise to hidden-layer activations. Establishes pruning can serve as a privacy mechanism, but does not propose a memorization-aware criterion.

related_work.title: Differential Privacy Meets Neural Network Pruning
related_work.relevance: Uses standard pruning to reduce DP-SGD dimensionality. We instead design a pruning criterion specifically optimized for memorization removal.

related_work.title: Estimating Example Difficulty Using Variance of Gradients
related_work.authors: Chirag Agarwal, Daniel D'souza, Sara Hooker
related_work.relevance: Establishes gradient variance (VoG) as a per-sample memorization metric across training epochs. We adapt this from sample domain to weight domain at a single checkpoint.

related_work.title: Against Membership Inference Attack: Pruning is All You Need
related_work.authors: Yijue Wang, Chenghong Wang, Zigeng Wang, Shanglin Zhou, Hang Liu, Jinbo Bi, Caiwen Ding, Sanguthevar Rajasekaran
related_work.relevance: Shows aggressive one-shot pruning can reduce MIA vulnerability at significant accuracy cost. Standard magnitude criterion; our method provides targeted memorization removal with better accuracy preservation.

related_work.title: Coherent Gradients: An Approach to Understanding Generalization in Gradient Descent-Based Optimization
related_work.relevance: Proposes the Coherent Gradients Hypothesis: SGD gradients are strongest in coherent directions reducing loss on many examples. Provides theoretical foundation for GDS---high per-weight gradient variance indicates incoherent (memorization-specific) updates.

related_work.title: Improving Generalization by Controlling Label-Noise Information in Neural Network Weights
related_work.authors: Hrayr Harutyunyan, Kyle Reing, Greg Ver Steeg, Aram Galstyan
related_work.relevance: Shows mutual information I(W;Y|X) between weights and training labels upper-bounds memorization and controls generalization. Provides information-theoretic justification for pruning high-GDS weights.

related_work.title: Variance-Based Pruning for Accelerating and Compressing Trained Networks
related_work.authors: Uranik Berisha, Jens Mehnert, Alexandru Paul Condurache
related_work.relevance: Uses activation variance for structured neuron pruning for compression. Different signal, direction, granularity, and objective from our per-sample gradient variance for privacy.

related_work.title: Membership Inference Attacks From First Principles
related_work.authors: Nicholas Carlini, Steve Chien, Milad Nasr, Shuang Song, Andreas Terzis, Florian Tramer
related_work.relevance: Introduces LiRA, the gold-standard MIA method we use for privacy evaluation.

related_work.title: Weak and Strong Gradient Directions: Explaining Memorization, Generalization, and Hardness of Examples at Scale
related_work.authors: Piotr Zielinski, Shankar Krishnan, Satrajit Chatterjee
related_work.relevance: Validates the Coherent Gradients Hypothesis at ImageNet scale, showing that suppressing incoherent gradient directions reduces memorization. Supports our approach of pruning weights with incoherent (high-variance) gradients.

related_work.title: Does Learning Require Memorization? A Short Tale about a Long Tail
related_work.relevance: Shows some memorization is necessary for generalization on long-tail distributions. We address this concern with per-class accuracy analysis.

hypothesis: Per-weight gradient dispersion (variance of per-sample gradients at a single checkpoint) reliably indicates memorization contribution, as predicted by the Coherent Gradients Hypothesis: weights with high gradient dispersion serve incoherent, sample-specific updates rather than generalizable patterns. Pruning these high-GDS weights removes memorization-specific capacity, yielding models that are simultaneously more compressed and less vulnerable to membership inference attacks compared to standard magnitude pruning, without disproportionately harming accuracy on rare classes. This can be validated with ground-truth memorized samples via label-noise canary injection.

primary: MemPrune reduces MIA AUC by at least 3-5 percentage points vs. magnitude pruning at 90% sparsity while maintaining within 1% test accuracy
MemPrune + DP-SGD outperforms magnitude pruning + DP-SGD at the same epsilon in test accuracy
GDS correlates with canary contribution ratio (Spearman rho > 0.3) in label-noise validation experiment
Per-class accuracy on CIFAR-100 shows MemPrune does not cause >2% additional degradation on bottom-10 classes vs. magnitude pruning
secondary: MemPrune at high sparsity achieves MIA resistance comparable to moderate-epsilon DP-SGD without formal DP mechanism
Consistent privacy improvement on ImageNet-100 demonstrating practical relevance beyond CIFAR
refutation: GDS shows no correlation with canary contribution ratio (ground-truth memorization)
Magnitude pruning already achieves low MIA rates at high sparsity (no room for improvement)
MemPrune causes >3% accuracy degradation compared to magnitude pruning
MemPrune disproportionately harms rare classes (>5% accuracy drop on bottom-10 CIFAR-100 classes vs. magnitude pruning)

compute_requirements: 1x NVIDIA RTX A6000 (48GB), ~7.5 hours total. VGG-16 per-sample gradients computed in chunks of 16 samples (~10GB peak GPU memory). ImageNet-100 with ResNet-18 only.
